---
title: "Session 5 · One-Way ANOVA"
---

## Overview

Once a single 0/1 dummy can compare two groups, what about three or more? In this session we represent a categorical predictor with a *set* of dummies and find that the resulting regression is exactly one-way ANOVA --- the classic test for comparing several group means. The omnibus *F* test asks whether the groups differ at all, and multiple comparisons then locate *where* those differences lie, carrying the course's "specific → general" arc one step further.

## Learning objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

- Encode a categorical predictor with three or more levels as a set of dummy variables.
- Interpret each dummy coefficient as a contrast against the reference group.
- Conduct and interpret the omnibus *F* test for equality of group means.
- Explain why one-way ANOVA is a special case of multiple regression.
- Apply a multiple-comparison procedure and explain why it controls the family-wise error rate.

## This session's materials

- {{< fa book-open-reader >}} **Read:** [Chapter 3: One-Way ANOVA](/notes/chapters/03-one-way-anova.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa laptop-code >}} **Lab:** [Lab 4: One-Way ANOVA](/labs/labs/lab04-categorical-contrasts.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa pencil >}} **Homework due:** [Homework 3: Confounding & Multiple Regression](/assignment/hw03.html)
- {{< fa circle-question >}} **Practice:** [Practice Quiz 3](/practice/chapters/quiz3-confounding-ancova.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa circle-check >}} **Graded quiz:** [Module Quiz 3](/assignment/quizzes.html#quiz-3)

## Key concepts

| Concept | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **One-way ANOVA** | A method for comparing the means of three or more groups defined by a single categorical factor. |
| **Set of dummies** | The collection of 0/1 indicators that together encode a *k*-level factor using *k* − 1 columns. |
| **Omnibus *F* test** | A single test of the null hypothesis that all group means are equal. |
| **Between-group variance** | Variation in the outcome explained by differences among the group means. |
| **Within-group variance** | Residual variation in the outcome left unexplained within each group. |
| **Multiple comparisons** | Follow-up tests that identify which specific pairs of group means differ. |
| **Family-wise error rate** | The probability of at least one false positive across a family of comparisons. |
| **ANOVA–regression equivalence** | The fact that one-way ANOVA is a regression on a set of dummy predictors. |
